aliases: Grilled steak with black pepper sauce

english: Grilled steak with black pepper sauce

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (beef meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for black pepper steak as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for black pepper steak as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of black pepper steak is approximately 200 g, comprising a grilled beef steak portion of roughly 150–170 g with black pepper sauce served on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Black Pepper Steak — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Black Pepper Steak (cr_id F06040011). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for black pepper steak as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors being the black pepper sauce (starch thickeners and minor sugar). The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 10.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The GI of mixed meat-and-sauce dishes with moderate starch content is estimated at approximately 40–50 per University of Sydney GI Database analogous mixed dish entries. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (45 × 10.33 × 2.0) / 100 = 9.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 181 mg/100 g; restaurant portions with full sauce may significantly increase sodium intake
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request black pepper sauce on the side and limit portion size to 150 g to keep sodium intake below 300 mg per meal from this dish; estimated potassium at 280 mg/100 g provides a moderately favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (10.33 g/100 g) and low sugar (1.94 g/100 g); protein moderates postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black pepper steak is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when sauce is controlled; the low carbohydrate load (GI ~45, GL ~9.3 per 200 g serving) and moderate protein content slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose excursions, but sauce should be requested on the side to avoid hidden starch or sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.2 g/100 g and estimated trans fat 0.1 g/100 g from natural bovine sources
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit black pepper steak to once or twice per week; saturated fat at ~4.2 g/100 g contributes to LDL elevation, and the estimated natural trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) adds marginal cardiovascular risk; preparation with olive oil instead of butter and sauce on the side is recommended to reduce saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6); saturated fat ~4.2 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g amplify pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption to no more than 1–2 times per week; the arachidonic acid content of beef amplifies inflammatory eicosanoid production, and the saturated and trans fat profile further promotes a pro-inflammatory state; preparation with olive oil and sauce on the side is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Pepper Steak is a Western-style grilled beef steak dish served with a black pepper sauce, commonly found in Singaporean Western food stalls, casual dining restaurants, and hawker-style Western cuisine outlets. The steak is typically made from a cut such as sirloin, ribeye, or rump, grilled or pan-seared to the desired doneness, and accompanied by a rich, peppery sauce made from crushed black peppercorns, beef stock, butter or cream, and sometimes starch thickeners. The dish has a savoury, slightly spicy, and umami-forward flavour profile with a tender to chewy texture depending on the cut and cooking time. Per 100 g, the dish provides 179 kcal, 10.31 g protein, 10.86 g fat (approximately 4.2 g saturated), 10.33 g carbohydrate, 1.94 g sugar, 1.67 g dietary fibre, and 181 mg sodium. The carbohydrate content is primarily from the sauce rather than the steak itself. Clinically, the dish is low in carbohydrates and suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when sauce is controlled; sodium is moderate and warrants caution for hypertension patients; saturated fat and natural trans fat content make it a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease; and its red meat and arachidonic acid profile makes it unsuitable for frequent consumption by rheumatoid arthritis patients. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. It is commonly served with sides such as coleslaw, fries, or mixed vegetables in the Singaporean Western food context.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database canonical entry for Black Pepper Steak (cr_id F06040011) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The dish is a well-defined Western-style beef steak preparation with a black pepper sauce, and the HPB entry is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef data as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked beef (USDA FDC ID 174032, beef sirloin, cooked) as the HPB Foodo entry does not separately report fatty acid fractions. These values are consistent with the total fat of 10.86 g/100 g reported by HPB. 2. Potassium (280 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked beef as HPB does not report potassium for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions beyond saturated fat are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database for this entry, and the USDA SR Legacy analogue does not provide a sufficiently close match for the composite dish including sauce. 4. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this composite dish entry, and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue was available to provide defensible estimates for the full composite. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database mixed dish entries; no direct GI measurement for black pepper steak exists in the approved GI database. 6. This dish is classified as Western cuisine; multilingual naming requirements apply only to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 7.2. Black pepper steak is a Western dish served in Singapore and does not trigger the Singaporean-dish multilingual requirement. Local-language names are therefore null. 7. The occasion_tag 'breakfast' from the input has been omitted from meal_context as black pepper steak is not a conventional breakfast item; it is retained in the input record but not propagated to the output meal_context.
